# Extractor Receipt

## Summary

This source contains empirical evidence about coding-agent adoption on GitHub in 2025, including estimated adoption rates, sampling and detection methodology, distribution across project contexts, contribution-size comparisons, commit-type distributions, time-trend observations, and explicit limitations about undercounting and partial observability.

## Evidence Files

### file: evidence-01.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-027
evidence_id: evidence-01
kind: metric
citation: Robbes et al. 2025, Abstract
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2601.18341
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The study reports an estimated coding-agent adoption rate on GitHub of 15.85% to 22.60% across 129,134 projects.

## Source Excerpt

“We leverage these traces to present the first large-scale study (129,134 projects) of the adoption of coding agents on GitHub, finding an estimated adoption rate of 15.85%–22.60%.”

## Note

This is an overall estimate derived from heuristic signals rather than a direct census of all coding-agent usage.
```

### file: evidence-02.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-027
evidence_id: evidence-02
kind: metric
citation: Robbes et al. 2025, Sec. 5.1
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2601.18341
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
File-level heuristics identified 10,191 of 129,134 projects as referencing coding-agent files or ignored coding-agent files, corresponding to 7.89%.

## Source Excerpt

“Summing both together, we find that 10,191 (8,257 + 1,934) projects reference coding agent files in their repositories, or 7.89%.”

## Note

This combines visible agent-related files with projects mentioning such files only in `.gitignore`.
```

### file: evidence-03.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-027
evidence_id: evidence-03
kind: metric
citation: Robbes et al. 2025, Sec. 5.2
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2601.18341
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
Among a sample of 15,783 projects with no file-level adoption signal, 1,364 projects showed commit-level adoption, or 8.64%.

## Source Excerpt

“Out of our sample, we find that 1,364, or 8.64% are commit-level adopters. Surprisingly, this is a higher percentage than the percentage of projects presenting file-level adoption.”

## Note

This creates tension with file-level detection because commit-level adoption appears in projects that show no file-level evidence.
```

### file: evidence-04.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-027
evidence_id: evidence-04
kind: method
citation: Robbes et al. 2025, Sec. 4.1
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2601.18341
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
The detection pipeline used four heuristic categories: file-based, author-based, branch-based, and label-based heuristics.

## Source Excerpt

“We employ a multi–faceted approach to identify repositories that use software‑engineering (SE) agents such as Copilot, Claude, Codex, and others. The heuristics are grouped into four categories: (1) File‑based ... (2) Author-based ... (3) Branch‑based ... (4) Label‑based ...”

## Note

The source explicitly frames these as heuristics rather than perfect detectors, so the evidence supports how detection was operationalized but also preserves uncertainty about coverage and false negatives.
```

### file: evidence-05.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-027
evidence_id: evidence-05
kind: claim-support
citation: Robbes et al. 2025, Sec. 4.1
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2601.18341
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
The authors excluded `CONVENTIONS.md` and treated `AGENTS.md` as insufficient for agent-specific identification because these files were too ambiguous for reliable attribution.

## Source Excerpt

“Because the risk of misclassifying such files outweighs the potential underestimation of Aider usage, we have excluded CONVENTIONS.md from our final heuristic set. Similarly, the AGENTS.md file was originally introduced by Codex; however, it is now used by dozens of coding agents... Consequently, the presence of this file alone is insufficient to reliably identify the coding agent in use.”

## Note

This evidence attaches an explicit caveat: some plausible traces were intentionally excluded or down-weighted to avoid false positives, which limits attribution precision.
```

### file: evidence-06.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-027
evidence_id: evidence-06
kind: metric
citation: Robbes et al. 2025, Sec. 6.2, Table 3
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2601.18341
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
File-level adoption was much higher in the youngest projects: 21.17% in the youngest age decile versus 4.69% in the oldest age decile.

## Source Excerpt

“Age (years) ... File 21.17% 9.72% 8.42% 6.56% 6.31% 6.28% 5.68% 5.18% 4.94% 4.69%” and “Younger projects have a far higher adoption rate than older projects, with a factor of 4 difference between the smallest decile ... and the largest.”

## Note

This shows a strong age gradient, while the paper also states that older projects still show non-negligible adoption.
```

### file: evidence-07.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-027
evidence_id: evidence-07
kind: claim-support
citation: Robbes et al. 2025, Sec. 7.1
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2601.18341
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
Among repositories from the top twenty organizations examined, file-level adoption was 10.18%, above the overall file-level adoption rate of 7.89%.

## Source Excerpt

“Over all the repositories in the top twenty organizations, file-level adoption is at 10.18%. For reference, the file-level adoption over all the repositories is 7.89%. This means that among open-source repositories of top organizations, there is a higher adoption of coding agents...”

## Note

The comparison is limited to public repositories associated with selected large organizations.
```

### file: evidence-08.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-027
evidence_id: evidence-08
kind: metric
citation: Robbes et al. 2025, Sec. 8.1
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2601.18341
confidence: medium
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The cumulative adoption curve showed a faster take-off at the end of February 2025, a further acceleration around mid-May 2025, and a slight slowdown by the end of August 2025.

## Source Excerpt

“At the end of February 2025, the slope increases, indicating a faster take-off... The slope increases further around mid-May 2025... Finally, adoption seems to slow down slightly at the end of August 2025.”

## Note

These are trend interpretations from the cumulative adoption curve rather than direct causal tests.
```

### file: evidence-09.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-027
evidence_id: evidence-09
kind: metric
citation: Robbes et al. 2025, Sec. 8.2
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2601.18341
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The top five coding agents accounted for more than 80% of observed tool adoption, and Claude plus Copilot alone accounted for more than half.

## Source Excerpt

“Figure 8 shows that a minority of tools show a significantly higher adoption than the rest. The top 5 tools ... account for more than 80% of the overall adoption. Just Claude and Copilot are responsible for more than half of the adoption.”

## Note

The paper treats the “Generic” AGENTS.md category as only partly attributable to Codex, so exact tool attribution remains partly uncertain.
```

### file: evidence-10.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-027
evidence_id: evidence-10
kind: metric
citation: Robbes et al. 2025, Sec. 9
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2601.18341
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
AI-assisted commits were larger than human-authored commits by median added lines: 34 lines for AI-assisted commits versus 10 for human commits.

## Source Excerpt

“For instance, the median number of added lines for a human contribution is 10... on the other hand, for AI-assisted commits, it rises to 34, a value triple the median for humans...”

## Note

The comparison is based on commits from file-level adopters that also had commit-level adoption.
```

### file: evidence-11.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-027
evidence_id: evidence-11
kind: result
citation: Robbes et al. 2025, Sec. 10.3, Table 7
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2601.18341
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
In a random sample of 790 Claude Code commits, feature commits were 35.7% and fix commits were 29.9%, together making up about two thirds of the sample.

## Source Excerpt

“Table 7 ... feat 282 35.7% ... fix 236 29.9% ...” and “Commits of types feat and fix are the most frequent in our sample, accounting for two thirds of the analyzed commits...”

## Note

This qualitative task-type analysis is limited to Claude Code commits, not all coding agents.
```

### file: evidence-12.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-027
evidence_id: evidence-12
kind: incident
citation: Robbes et al. 2025, Sec. 11.3-11.4
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2601.18341
confidence: medium
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
The paper argues that coding-agent use is more likely undercounted than overcounted because traces can be hidden, omitted, or left outside repositories.

## Source Excerpt

“On the other hand, there are several reasons why we may under-estimate the use of coding agents.” “First, we think that projects adopting coding agents at the file level that have no AI-assisted commits are more likely to be using coding agents, rather than having abandoned them.” “Considering all the reasons for undercounting and overcounting AI adoption, we think it is more likely that we are undercounting it.”

## Note

This is the authors’ interpretation of study limitations rather than a directly measured quantity, and it depends on assumptions about hidden or manually committed agent usage.
---

Among repositories from the top twenty organizations examined, file-level adoption was 10.18%, above the overall file-level adoption rate of 7.89%.

## Source Excerpt

“Over all the repositories in the top twenty organizations, file-level adoption is at 10.18%. For reference, the file-level adoption over all the repositories is 7.89%. This means that among open-source repositories of top organizations, there is a higher adoption of coding agents...”

## Note

The comparison is limited to public repositories associated with selected large organizations.
```

---

contradiction_relevance: tension
---
The paper argues that coding-agent use is more likely undercounted than overcounted because traces can be hidden, omitted, or left outside repositories.

## Source Excerpt

“On the other hand, there are several reasons why we may under-estimate the use of coding agents.” “First, we think that projects adopting coding agents at the file level that have no AI-assisted commits are more likely to be using coding agents, rather than having abandoned them.” “Considering all the reasons for undercounting and overcounting AI adoption, we think it is more likely that we are undercounting it.”

## Note

This is the authors’ interpretation of study limitations rather than a directly measured quantity, and it depends on assumptions about hidden or manually committed agent usage.

# Extractor Receipt

## Summary

This source contains empirical evidence about coding-agent adoption on GitHub in 2025, including estimated adoption rates, sampling and detection methodology, distribution across project contexts, contribution-size comparisons, commit-type distributions, time-trend observations, and explicit limitations about undercounting and partial observability.

## Evidence Files

### file: evidence-01.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-027
evidence_id: evidence-01
kind: metric
citation: Robbes et al. 2025, Abstract
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2601.18341
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The study reports an estimated coding-agent adoption rate on GitHub of 15.85% to 22.60% across 129,134 projects.

## Source Excerpt

“We leverage these traces to present the first large-scale study (129,134 projects) of the adoption of coding agents on GitHub, finding an estimated adoption rate of 15.85%–22.60%.”

## Note

This is an overall estimate derived from heuristic signals rather than a direct census of all coding-agent usage.
```

### file: evidence-02.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-027
evidence_id: evidence-02
kind: metric
citation: Robbes et al. 2025, Sec. 5.1
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2601.18341
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
File-level heuristics identified 10,191 of 129,134 projects as referencing coding-agent files or ignored coding-agent files, corresponding to 7.89%.

## Source Excerpt

“Summing both together, we find that 10,191 (8,257 + 1,934) projects reference coding agent files in their repositories, or 7.89%.”

## Note

This combines visible agent-related files with projects mentioning such files only in `.gitignore`.
```

### file: evidence-03.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-027
evidence_id: evidence-03
kind: metric
citation: Robbes et al. 2025, Sec. 5.2
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2601.18341
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
Among a sample of 15,783 projects with no file-level adoption signal, 1,364 projects showed commit-level adoption, or 8.64%.

## Source Excerpt

“Out of our sample, we find that 1,364, or 8.64% are commit-level adopters. Surprisingly, this is a higher percentage than the percentage of projects presenting file-level adoption.”

## Note

This creates tension with file-level detection because commit-level adoption appears in projects that show no file-level evidence.
```

### file: evidence-04.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-027
evidence_id: evidence-04
kind: method
citation: Robbes et al. 2025, Sec. 4.1
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2601.18341
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
The detection pipeline used four heuristic categories: file-based, author-based, branch-based, and label-based heuristics.

## Source Excerpt

“We employ a multi–faceted approach to identify repositories that use software‑engineering (SE) agents such as Copilot, Claude, Codex, and others. The heuristics are grouped into four categories: (1) File‑based ... (2) Author-based ... (3) Branch‑based ... (4) Label‑based ...”

## Note

The source explicitly frames these as heuristics rather than perfect detectors, so the evidence supports how detection was operationalized but also preserves uncertainty about coverage and false negatives.
```

### file: evidence-05.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-027
evidence_id: evidence-05
kind: claim-support
citation: Robbes et al. 2025, Sec. 4.1
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2601.18341
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
The authors excluded `CONVENTIONS.md` and treated `AGENTS.md` as insufficient for agent-specific identification because these files were too ambiguous for reliable attribution.

## Source Excerpt

“Because the risk of misclassifying such files outweighs the potential underestimation of Aider usage, we have excluded CONVENTIONS.md from our final heuristic set. Similarly, the AGENTS.md file was originally introduced by Codex; however, it is now used by dozens of coding agents... Consequently, the presence of this file alone is insufficient to reliably identify the coding agent in use.”

## Note

This evidence attaches an explicit caveat: some plausible traces were intentionally excluded or down-weighted to avoid false positives, which limits attribution precision.
```

File-level adoption was much higher in the youngest projects: 21.17% in the youngest age decile versus 4.69% in the oldest age decile.

## Source Excerpt

“Age (years) ... File 21.17% 9.72% 8.42% 6.56% 6.31% 6.28% 5.68% 5.18% 4.94% 4.69%” and “Younger projects have a far higher adoption rate than older projects, with a factor of 4 difference between the smallest decile ... and the largest.”

## Note

This shows a strong age gradient, while the paper also states that older projects still show non-negligible adoption.
```
